passages.explanation: Jesus travels through towns and villages, proclaiming the good news of the kingdom of God. The Twelve are with him, along with some women who've been cured of evil spirits and diseases: Mary called Magdalene, from whom seven demons had come out; Joanna the wife of Chuza, the manager of Herod's household; Susanna; and many others. These women are supporting Jesus and the Twelve out of their own means. Luke is the only Gospel writer who highlights this — the ministry is financially supported by women, some of them wealthy and well-connected. Joanna has a direct link to Herod's court.

A large crowd gathers, and Jesus tells the parable of the sower. A farmer scatters seed: some falls on the path and is trampled or eaten by birds. Some falls on rocky ground, sprouts quickly, and withers for lack of moisture. Some falls among thorns, which choke it as it grows. Some falls on good soil, comes up, and yields a crop a hundred times more than was sown. Jesus calls out: whoever has ears to hear, let them hear. His disciples ask what the parable means. He explains: the seed is the word of God. The path represents those who hear, then the devil takes the word from their hearts so they can't believe and be saved. The rocky ground: those who receive the word with joy but have no root — they believe for a while but fall away when tested. The thorns: those who hear but are choked by life's worries, riches, and pleasures, and don't mature. The good soil: those with a noble and good heart who hear the word, retain it, and by persevering produce a crop. Four types of soil, one type of seed. The variable isn't the message — it's the reception.

No one lights a lamp and hides it or puts it under a bed. Instead they put it on a stand so that those who come in can see the light. There is nothing hidden that won't be disclosed, nothing concealed that won't be known and brought into the open. Therefore consider carefully how you listen — whoever has will be given more, and whoever does not have, even what they think they have will be taken from them. The warning connects directly to the parable of the sower: how you receive the word matters. Passive hearing leads to loss. Active engagement leads to growth.

Jesus' mother and brothers come to see him but can't get through the crowd. Someone tells him: your mother and brothers are standing outside, wanting to see you. Jesus replies: my mother and brothers are those who hear God's word and put it into practice. Jesus isn't rejecting his family — he's expanding the definition. Biological ties don't create spiritual priority. Obedience does.

One day Jesus gets into a boat with his disciples and says: let's go over to the other side of the lake. They set out. As they sail, he falls asleep. A squall comes down on the lake and the boat is swamped — they're in great danger. The disciples wake him: master, master, we're going to drown! He gets up, rebukes the wind and the raging waters. The storm subsides and it's calm. He asks them: where is your faith? In fear and amazement they ask each other: who is this? He commands even the winds and the water, and they obey him. The Old Testament is clear about who commands the sea — God does (Psalm 89:9, Psalm 107:29). The disciples' question is the right one.

They sail to the region of the Gerasenes, across from Galilee. A demon-possessed man from the town meets Jesus — he's been naked, living among the tombs, and hasn't lived in a house for a long time. When he sees Jesus, he falls down and shouts: what do you want with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? I beg you, don't torture me! Jesus has been commanding the impure spirit to come out. The man has been chained hand and foot and kept under guard, but he breaks the chains and is driven by the demon into solitary places. Jesus asks his name. 'Legion,' he replies — because many demons have gone into him. They beg Jesus not to send them into the Abyss. A large herd of pigs is feeding on the hillside. The demons beg to be sent into the pigs. Jesus permits it. The demons enter the pigs, the herd rushes down the steep bank into the lake and drowns. The herdsmen flee and report it. People come and find the man sitting at Jesus' feet, dressed and in his right mind — and they're afraid. The witnesses describe what happened. The whole crowd from the region asks Jesus to leave because they're overcome with fear. Jesus gets into the boat. The man begs to go with him, but Jesus sends him home: return and tell how much God has done for you. He goes away and tells the whole town what Jesus has done. The people prefer the predictable danger of a demoniac over the unpredictable power of Jesus. They'd rather keep their pigs than keep the healer.

When Jesus returns, a crowd welcomes him. A man named Jairus, a synagogue leader, falls at Jesus' feet and pleads with him to come to his house — his only daughter, about twelve, is dying. As Jesus goes, the crowds almost crush him. A woman who has been subject to bleeding for twelve years and can't be healed by anyone comes up behind him and touches the edge of his cloak. Immediately her bleeding stops. Jesus asks: who touched me? Everyone denies it. Peter says: master, the people are crowding and pressing against you. But Jesus says: someone touched me — I know that power has gone out from me. The woman, seeing she can't go unnoticed, comes trembling, falls at his feet, and tells everyone why she touched him and how she was instantly healed. Jesus says: daughter, your faith has healed you. Go in peace. She's been ritually unclean for twelve years — excluded from the temple, from community life. Jesus makes her tell her story publicly, not to shame her but to restore her. The whole community needs to know she's clean.

While Jesus is still speaking, someone from Jairus' house arrives: your daughter is dead. Don't bother the teacher any more. Jesus hears this and tells Jairus: don't be afraid. Just believe, and she will be healed. At the house, he allows only Peter, John, James, and the girl's parents to enter. Everyone is wailing and mourning. Jesus says: stop wailing. She's not dead but asleep. They laugh at him — they know she's dead. He takes her by the hand and says: my child, get up! Her spirit returns and she stands up immediately. He tells them to give her something to eat and orders them not to tell anyone what happened. Jesus heals a woman who's been suffering for twelve years, then raises a girl who is twelve years old. The number connects the stories — the woman's suffering has lasted as long as the girl has been alive, and both end on the same day.
